title: A reason why we do not observe Schrödinger's cats
abstract: A reason is discussed (may be not the only one) for why we do not see any superposition of macroscopic states in the real world. Under the general assumption that quantum macrostates are statistical ensembles of microstates, it is shown that any superposition of macrostates is reduced in a very short time by the unitary dynamics of the ordinary Schrödinger equation, deducing the Born rule without having to postulate it. In more detail, the macroscopic and microscopic degrees of freedom are decoupled in the Schrödinger equation, yielding an effective stochastic equation for the macroscopic variables, with the ensemble average of the microscopic amplitudes that acts as a self-generated internal white noise. The stochastic equation is shown to be a reducing Itô equation if some general causality conditions are met, predicting a very quick collapse of any macroscopic superposition upon formation, with probabilities which satisfy the Born rule. In the context of the von Neumann measurement scheme, the relevance of the result is discussed as a simple dynamical solution of the measurement problem.
summary: This paper proposes a dynamical resolution to the quantum measurement problem by showing that the standard Schrödinger equation leads to a stochastic equation for macroscopic variables. By analyzing this equation using Itô calculus, the authors demonstrate that any macroscopic superposition will rapidly collapse into a definite state, thereby deriving the Born rule without explicit postulation.
main_problem: The paper addresses the measurement problem by providing a dynamical reason why macroscopic quantum superpositions, such as Schrödinger's cat, are not observed in reality.
main_result: The standard Schrödinger equation, when analyzed through a stochastic framework, predicts that any macroscopic superposition will collapse almost instantaneously, and this collapse process naturally yields the Born rule.
method: The analysis involves decoupling macroscopic and microscopic degrees of freedom and deriving a stochastic differential equation (SDE) for the macroscopic amplitudes, which is then analyzed using Itô calculus.
model_or_system: The model uses the standard Schrödinger equation for a quantum system treated as a superposition of macrostates. The dynamics are mapped onto an effective stochastic equation whose noise term is self-generated by the microscopic ensemble average.
key_observables: The decay of off-diagonal coherence terms ($\langle p_k(t) p_m(t) angle$) and the recovery of the Born rule ($\langle p_m(t) angle = p_m(0)$).
important_parameters: The ratio of the inertial relaxation time ($	au_R$) to the noise correlation time ($	au_c$), and the macroscopic constraint $\Delta E \Delta X \gg \hbar c$.
main_assumptions: The core assumption is that the macroscopic state can be treated as a statistical ensemble of microstates, and the derivation relies on the white noise approximation for the random matrix elements.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by deriving a stochastic equation from the Schrödinger dynamics, analyzing the required mathematical interpretation (Itô vs. Stratonovich) based on physical time scales, and finally demonstrating that the Itô interpretation leads to rapid collapse consistent with observation.
why_interesting: This work offers a non-standard, dynamical approach to the measurement problem, providing a mechanism rooted in statistical physics (stochastic processes) that naturally enforces the Born rule for macroscopic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
